title: Improved Dual Attack and Trapdoor Sampling via Quantum Rejection Sampling
authors: Cong Ling, Hao Yan, Nicholas Zhao
abstract: In this work, we revisit the dual attack and GPV trapdoor sampling, focusing on the lattice Gaussian sampling term, which can be a significant bottleneck in the overall complexity. We show that this sampling step can be quantumly accelerated by combining the lower bound underlying Wang and Ling's analysis of Klein's algorithm with the quantum rejection sampling (QRS) framework proposed by Ozols et al. Specifically, this lower bound gives precisely the pointwise domination condition required for quantum rejection sampling when given coherent oracle access to a truncated Klein proposal distribution, which yields a quantum procedure for preparing the truncated dual $q$-ary lattice Gaussian with a quadratic reduction in the sampling complexity. The truncation radius is chosen so that the truncated distribution is negligibly close to the full lattice Gaussian in total variation distance. Substituting this sampler into the dual attack framework results in reduced overall attack-cost estimates. Compared with Pouly and Shen's modern dual attack under the same parameter choices, our estimates reduce the attack cost by \(9\), \(4\), and \(13\) bits for Kyber-512, Kyber-768, and Kyber-1024, respectively. We also report the corresponding estimates with modulus switching. Finally, by replacing the Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler with the QRS algorithm, we achieve a similar quadratic speedup in the GPV signing process.
summary: This paper introduces a quantum algorithm to accelerate the discrete Gaussian sampling required for lattice-based cryptographic attacks. By using quantum rejection sampling, the authors achieve a quadratic speedup in the sampling process, leading to lower estimated security levels for post-quantum standards like Kyber.
main_problem: The Gaussian sampling step in lattice-based cryptographic attacks (dual attack) and GPV trapdoor sampling is a significant computational bottleneck that remains classical.
main_result: The authors develop a quantum algorithm using Quantum Rejection Sampling (QRS) that achieves a quadratic speedup in sampling complexity, reducing the estimated attack costs for Kyber variants by up to 13 bits.
method: The method combines the lower bound of Klein's algorithm with the Quantum Rejection Sampling framework to prepare truncated lattice Gaussian states using amplitude amplification.
model_or_system: The work focuses on lattice-based cryptographic primitives, specifically the Learning with Errors (LWE) and Short Integer Solution (SIS) problems involving q-ary lattices and their duals.
key_observables: Attack cost estimates (in bits), total variation distance between truncated and full distributions, and the empirical cosine score for identifying secret vectors.
important_parameters: The Gaussian parameter (s), the truncation radius (R), the sampling ratio (Delta_R), and the Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization profile (Delta).
main_assumptions: The algorithm assumes access to a coherent oracle for a truncated Klein proposal distribution and requires a truncation radius that ensures negligible error in total variation distance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the concentration of discrete Gaussian mass for different Gaussian parameters; Figure 2 illustrates the oracle-level decomposition of the QRS circuit; Figure 3 shows the exponential gap between MCMC and QRS upper bounds.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the bottleneck in lattice sampling, proposes a QRS-based framework using a truncated Klein proposal, provides mathematical proofs for complexity reduction and error bounds, and evaluates the impact on Kyber and FALCON security parameters.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 2: Oracle-level decomposition of our quantum sampler. The top wire is the coefficient register X, and the bottom wire is the coin register. The first two gates AR = Ra(OQR ⊗I0) prepare the state for QRS. The operator UR is one iterate of the amplitude amplification subroutine, whose dashed box gives its full decomposition. This is repeated O  1 √pR
Fig. 3: MCMC and QRS upper bounds derived from [29]’s ϑ3(γ)n estimate for FALCON-512. The horizontal axis uses the Gaussian parameter s, with γ = (s/Bmax)2.
